title: Turning a cold compost heap
subtitle: When to turn, what to check for, and how to speed up a slow pile without a full rebuild
excerpt: A cold compost heap decomposes slowly but reliably without turning. Turning it once or twice a year can double or triple the decomposition rate by introducing oxygen, redistributing moisture, and exposing undecomposed outer material. This is the technique for assessing a cold heap and deciding whether, how, and when to turn it.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic 'Cold composting' factsheet. RHS 'Composting: how to make compost' guidance. WRAP Home Composting evidence report (2009). Rodale Book of Composting on turning frequency effects.

glossaryTerms.definition: Compost produced by slow, ambient-temperature decomposition without the active management (turning, moisture control, C:N balancing) that produces a hot heap. Cold composting takes 12 to 24 months but requires minimal effort and produces good finished compost.

A long, low pile of composting material, typically with a triangular cross-section. Turning a single-bay heap into a windrow-style pile, or forking it from one bay to another, is the standard method for introducing air and repositioning the material.

content.text: heap left entirely alone will produce finished compost, but it takes 18 to 24 months. A single turn in the spring speeds the process to 12 to 15 months by introducing oxygen and moving the undecomposed dry outer material to the moist centre. Two turns per year (spring and autumn) can achieve the same result in 8 to 10 months. This approaches hot-compost timescales without the effort of weekly turning.
Spring (March to April): the best time. Temperatures are rising, the heap has had a winter to break down the most recent additions, and turning at this point allows the heap to work through summer.

Autumn (October): useful if the heap looks slow. The turn exposes and aerates material before winter slows decomposition. Do not turn in late autumn after temperatures drop consistently below 10°C; the benefit is minimal.
When it smells or looks wrong: if the heap is wet and slimy, dry and powdery, or has obvious mat layers, turn and correct the cause.

Moisture: squeeze a handful from the centre. It should feel like a wrung-out sponge. Dry centres need watering before and during the turn. Wet heaps need dry carbon material (shredded card, wood chip) mixed in.
Maturity: if there is finished dark compost at the bottom, extract it before turning. Use it, and use the turn to separate raw material from finished material.
Mat layers: compacted lawn clippings or wet newspaper form anaerobic mats. Fork these apart during the turn and mix with carbon material.
How to turn a single-bin cold heap
If you have two bays, fork the entire contents from bay 1 to bay 2, forming a
shape. What was on the outside goes in the middle of the new pile. What was at the bottom goes near the top. Water if dry.
If you have only one bay, fork the contents out onto the ground beside the bin, turning the pile inside-out as you go, then fork back in. The outer dry material should now be in the centre.
Add carbon material if the contents look wet or dark: one bucket of wood chip or torn cardboard per two buckets of compost.

Cover the turned pile to retain moisture and warmth. After a spring turn, check the heap again in 4 to 6 weeks: the material should look darker and more uniform. In a good spring, a turned heap can reach 40 to 50°C briefly at the centre as microbial activity picks up, even without full hot-compost management.
